title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What Is a Convertible Term Life Insurance Policy?
Life doesn’t always go as planned, so having flexible options can really help. That’s what a Convertible Term Life Insurance plan gives you. It starts out as a regular term plan that offers basic life cover for a certain period. But here’s the great part—you can later switch it to a plan that also helps you save money, like an endowment plan, and you don’t even need to take a medical test again. It’s a smart choice for anyone who wants to begin with simple protection and add more benefits later. In this blog, we’ll walk you through how these plans work, why they’re useful, and how they compare to other types of life insurance. So whether you’re just starting to look into insurance or want something that can change as your life does, you’re in the right place.
Features of Convertible Term Life Insurance Plans A convertible term life insurance plan is a type of term policy that allows you to change your plan later. You can convert it into another type of life insurance plan like an endowment plan, without undergoing a medical test. This plan is useful for people who want simple life cover now but may want savings benefits later. You start with a basic plan and change it later when your needs grow.
On-Demand Conversion (Explained Simply)
A convertible term life insurance policy lets you change your plan later—but only if you ask for it.
The policy doesn’t change by itself.
You must send a written request to your insurer.
If you don't ask for the change, the plan continues as a term policy and ends when you either complete the policy term or in case of your unfortunate death.
This gives you control over when and if you want to switch to a new type of policy.
Premiums Premium is the amount of money you pay for your insurance.
It is decided when the policy begins and stays fixed throughout the policy term.
Your premium amount depends on several factors such as:
Your age at the time of purchase
The coverage amount (sum assured)
The policy term and the premium payment term (how long you’ll pay)
The insurance company uses these details to calculate a rate that stays the same for the full duration of the policy.
Planning ahead becomes easier because you know exactly how much you need to pay.
The best part? Even if you convert the plan to an endowment or whole life policy later, your premium stays the same.
This makes convertible term plans a budget-friendly option with long-term flexibility.
Sum AssuredSum assured is the amount your family will get if something happens to you.
Convertible term plans usually offer a moderate sum assured to start with.
The reason for this is flexibility—since you might convert the plan later into something that includes savings and a maturity benefit.
These plans strike a balance:
They give you enough coverage now to protect your loved ones.
And later, if you convert, the same plan can help you build savings or receive a maturity benefit.
In short, the insurance company keeps it balanced—some life cover now, with a chance to grow benefits later.
Riders (Explained Simply)
Riders are extra protections you can add to your plan for a small extra cost. Some common riders are:
Critical Illness Rider – Helps if you're diagnosed with a major illness like cancer.
Accidental Death Benefit Rider – Gives your family extra money if death is due to an accident.
Waiver of Premium Rider – Stops future premium payments if you become seriously ill or disabled. These riders give added safety and peace of mind to you and your family.
Taxation (Explained Simply)
Your convertible term plan also helps you save on taxes:
The money you pay as premium can be claimed for tax deduction under Section 80C, up to Rs. 1.5 lakhs in a financial year, under old tax regime.
The money your family receives as a death benefit (or you receive as maturity benefit after conversion) is tax-free under Section 10(10D) subject to certain conditions. This makes the plan financially smart as well as secure.
A Two-in-One Benefit (Explained Simply)
Convertible term plans give you the best of both worlds:
It starts as a term plan that gives pure life cover.
Later, you can switch it into an endowment plan, which adds savings + maturity benefit.
You don’t even need a medical test to make this switch. It’s like starting with a simple phone and upgrading it to a smartphone—when you’re ready.
Features of Convertible Whole Life Assurance Policy
Covers you until age 80 or death.
You can convert it typically after 5 years (within the 6th year).
Bonus (if declared) and sum assured are paid on death or maturity.
Loan facility available after 4 years.
Can be surrendered after 3 years, but no bonus if before 5 years.
Bonus rate: Rs. 60 per Rs. 1000 (whole life), Rs. 48 per Rs. 1000 (endowment).
Age Limit for Convertible Whole Life Assurance
Entry age: 19 to 45 years.
Can be converted if insured is below 55 years on the date of conversion.
Disadvantage of a Whole Life Insurance Policy
Whole Life plans are more expensive than term plans.
They also have lower investment returns compared to mutual funds.
You may not get flexible premium or investment options.
The cash value builds slowly and is subject to policy rules.
Death Risk Covered by the Whole Life Plan
Covers till age 99 or 100.
If the life assured passes away during the term, nominee gets sum assured + bonuses (if any).
You must pay all premiums for the death benefit to apply.
Changing Convertible Policy to a Whole Life Policy
Regular term plans cannot be changed to whole life plans.
But convertible term plans can be changed to whole life or endowment plans.
You need to request the conversion.
If not requested within the set period, the plan remains unchanged.
Difference Between Convertible Term and Whole Life Insurance Plan
Conclusion
Convertible Term Life Insurance is designed to provide you with the flexibility that begins as life insurance and can later convert to a plan with savings elements. This flexibility is useful for you if the current need is for life insurance only, but you want to include maturity benefits at a future date. You will not need to undergo a medical examination at the time you convert to the new plan. The premium will be the same with these plans, and the coverage provides tax benefits that you will need to consider.
Whole Life Assurance types like Suvidha also provide a form of flexibility, but is typically more expensive. Both a Whole life assurance, and a term policy serve very different needs, and in case you are looking for that level of affordability and availability for future saving opportunities, then you may consider a convertible term plan.
FAQs How does a Convertible Term Plan differ from a Regular Term Plan?
A regular term plan gives you  life cover for a fixed time. A convertible term plan also starts with life cover, but you can change it later to a plan with maturity benefits without taking a medical test.
How does the conversion process work for a convertible term life insurance?
How does a Convertible Whole Life Assurance Policy work?
It starts as a whole life policy. After 5 years, you can convert it into an endowment plan. If you don’t convert within the 6th year, it remains a whole life plan. On maturity or death, the sum assured plus bonus (if declared) is paid.
What are the benefits of Convertible Term Life Insurance?
You get term insurance now and the option to switch to a savings-based plan later. Premiums remain fixed, no medical test is required, and you get tax benefits. It offers flexibility for future needs.
How does Convertible Term Life Insurance differ from Whole Life Insurance?
Convertible term insurance covers for a limited period and offers conversion to another plan. Whole life insurance gives lifelong cover with savings but has higher premiums.
